title: Joint Accounts vs. Individual Accounts: What Happens to Each
summary: The difference between joint and individual accounts significantly affects how quickly and simply money becomes accessible.
category: After a Loss III: Estate & Digital
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Joint Accounts vs. Individual Accounts: What Happens to Each | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: The difference between joint and individual accounts significantly affects how quickly and simply money becomes accessible.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Joint accounts with rights of survivorship generally pass directly to the surviving owner.
Individual accounts typically go through the estate or probate process.
Beneficiary designations (POD/TOD) can simplify individual accounts significantly.

paragraphs: Most joint bank accounts include automatic "rights of survivorship," meaning the funds pass directly to the surviving joint owner without probate — a notification and death certificate are usually all that's needed.

Individual accounts without a named beneficiary typically become part of the estate and may go through probate, which can take months. However, accounts with a "payable on death" (POD) or "transfer on death" (TOD) designation bypass this, passing directly to the named person.

nextSteps: Identify which accounts are joint versus individual.
Check whether individual accounts have POD/TOD designations already in place.
Consider adding POD/TOD designations to other accounts going forward, where appropriate.
disclaimer: General information, not legal advice — an estate attorney can clarify specific account treatment under your state's law.
